what: P0.1 — fixest/ivreg dispatch silently ignores fixed effects
title: Deep expert review of ivcheck
text: : R/dispatch.R:40 (y <- .numeric(stats::model.matrix(object, type = "lhs"))) R/dispatch.R:42 (d <- .numeric(fitted(fs) + residuals(fs))). Problem: user fitting feols(y ~ x | firm | d ~ z, data) declared within-firm variation identify . current dispatch extracts raw y raw d runs test un-demeaned data. verified directly: synthetic model strong firm FE gives var(extracted y) = 1.53 versus var(FE-demeaned y) = 1.10 — FE simply dropped. Impact: test answers different question (Z valid IV D pooled-across-firm population?) user asked (Z valid IV D partialling firm?). paper panel data get misleading conclusions. Fix (recommended v0.1.0): extract_iv_data.fixest, check length(object$fixef_vars) > 0; , abort clear message: Analogous check ivreg (doesn’t commonly use FE) — skip. Alternative v0.2.0: stratify test FE cell (run test within level combine p-values via Simes harmonic mean). genuine research question, bug fix, scope v0.1.0.
code: if (length(object$fixef_vars) > 0L) {   cli::cli_abort(c(     "{.fn ivcheck} v0.1.0 does not support models with fixed effects.",     i = "The discrete-Z tests of Kitagawa / Mourifié-Wan / FLL operate on the raw        (Y, D, Z) joint distribution; residualising on fixed effects destroys        the discrete structure of Z.",     i = "Workaround: for a FE-IV model, pre-demean (Y, D) within FE cells,        keep Z discrete, and call the default method on the vectors."   )) }

what: P0.2 — iv_testjfe multivalued D with covariates gives conservative σ²
title: Deep expert review of ivcheck
text: : R/iv_testjfe.R:200–209 (structural residual construction). Problem: FWL, WLS regression mu_j P_design (contains per-judge averages residualised level-indicators) produces β̂_ell coefficient residualised indicators, raw indicators. Therefore correct structural residual u_i = ỹ_i − α̂ − Σ_ell β̂_ell · ind_resid_ell(), u_i = ỹ_i − α̂ − β̂[D_raw_i]. two differ β̂ · (D_raw − D_resid), linear FE/X partialling β̂ · (+ b·x_i) within judge. non-zero within-judge variation. Impact: σ̂² = ss_within / df_within picks extra within-judge variation, σ̂² inflated, T_n / σ̂² deflated, χ² p-value larger (test conservative). Monte Carlo null size drifts nominal 5% M ≥ 2 x non-trivial variation within judges. binary D (M = 1) x, d_num overwritten residualised version line 137, beta_vec * d_num already uses residualised D. bug M = 1. Fix: multivalued--x branch, save per-observation residualised indicators P_design construction use u_i. Rough sketch around R/iv_testjfe.R:170–184: lines 200–209, multivalued branch becomes: restores FWL identity. Test add: H0 multivalued D (say M = 2) x varying within judges, χ² null size still nominal 5%. quick MC n_reps = 200 K = 10 judges catches broken.
code: } else {     beta_full <- numeric(length(d_vals))     beta_full[match(d_vals[-1L], d_vals)] <- beta_vec     idx_map <- match(d_num_raw, d_vals)     u_i <- y - alpha_hat - beta_full[idx_map]   # RAW indicators, not residualised } ind_resid_mat <- matrix(0, nrow = n, ncol = M)  # new for (m in seq_len(M)) {   dv <- d_vals[m + 1L]   ind <- as.numeric(d_num_raw == dv)   if (!is.null(x)) {     design <- cbind(1, x_mat)     ind <- as.numeric(stats::residuals(stats::lm.fit(design, ind)))   }   ind_resid_mat[, m] <- ind                    # save for structural residuals   P_design[, m + 1L] <- vapply(judges, ...) } } else {     u_i <- y - alpha_hat     for (m in seq_len(M)) u_i <- u_i - beta_vec[m] * ind_resid_mat[, m] }

what: P0.3 — validate_treatment_discrete remaps D, binding labels become wrong
title: Deep expert review of ivcheck
text: : R/utils.R:46–50. Problem: user passes D ∈ {1, 2, 3} (perfectly legitimate input), validator silently remaps {0, 1, 2}. Kitagawa core reports binding$direction = "D <= 0" user’s data D = 1 smallest level. user reads “D <= 0” thinks violation -treatment cell, really D = 1. Impact: cosmetic misleading. PhD student running real data integer-coded D notice immediately. Fix: return remap table validator use original d_vals direction_info() binding$direction. simpler: don’t remap unless necessary (remap values already contiguous--0 integer sequence). Actually simplest: kitagawa_core_test, constructing labels, use original d_vals rather remapped internal values. requires validator pass original levels, currently . Low-risk addition: validate_treatment_discrete return list (list(d_num = ..., d_vals = ...)) instead vector.
code: if (!all(vals == seq(0L, k - 1L))) {   mapping <- stats::setNames(seq(0L, k - 1L), vals)   d_num <- as.numeric(mapping[as.character(d_num)]) }

text: : tests/testthat/test-known-values.R. Problem: tests self-consistent constructions. never assert iv_kitagawa(card$lwage, card$college, card$near_college) matches rejection Kitagawa’s 2015 paper reports Card. reviewer ask “package reproduce Kitagawa / MW / FLL papers’ numbers?” don’t direct answer test suite. what: P1.6 — Bootstrap SE in MW is “frozen” from observed but recomputed per pair
title: Deep expert review of ivcheck
text: : R/mw_core.R:167–169. Problem (minor): CLR bootstrap canonically freezes studentisation observed plug-. code freeze (uses SE_on_grid, observed-data matrix, bootstrap re-estimate). Good. pmax(..., se_floor) application differs observed statistic (line 95, uses + se_floor additive) bootstrap (line 118 169, uses pmax(..., se_floor)). floors differ: sqrt(x) + se_floor vs max(sqrt(x), se_floor). Impact: ordinary case sqrt(qf) > se_floor, identical. sqrt(qf) < se_floor, observed uses sqrt(qf) + se_floor (roughly 2·se_floor) bootstrap uses se_floor. asymmetry biases bootstrap critical values slightly. Negligible cases fastidious reviewer might flag . Fix: harmonise R/mw_core.R:95 — change sqrt(pmax(qf, 0)) + se_floor pmax(sqrt(pmax(qf, 0)), se_floor).
code: se_low  <- SE_on_grid[, , k_low,  d_idx] se_high <- SE_on_grid[, , k_high, d_idx] SE_pair <- pmax(sqrt(se_low^2 + se_high^2), se_floor)

title: Getting started with ivcheck
text: ivcheck tests identifying assumptions instrumental variable (IV) models: local exclusion restriction monotonicity conditions required LATE identification (Imbens Angrist 1994). package implements three tests methodological literature share assumptions null: iv_kitagawa(): Kitagawa (2015), binary treatment discrete instrument. iv_mw(): Mourifie Wan (2017), case plus covariates. iv_testjfe(): Frandsen, Lefgren, Leslie (2023), judge-fixed-effects design instrument set mutually exclusive dummies. also one-shot wrapper iv_check() inspects fitted IV model runs every applicable test. vignette walks raw-vector interface. See vignette("-fixest") fitted-model interface vignette("judge-designs") judge case.

title: Getting started with ivcheck
text: first stage Pr(D = 1 | Z = 1) - Pr(D = 1 | Z = 0) = 0.4 strong. Y depends observed treatment, IV valid construction. test reject.
code: library(ivcheck)  set.seed(1) n <- 500 z <- sample(0:1, n, replace = TRUE) d <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.3 + 0.4 * z) y <- rnorm(n, mean = d)  result <- iv_kitagawa(y, d, z, n_boot = 500, parallel = FALSE) print(result) #>  #> ── Kitagawa (2015) ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Sample size: 500 #> Statistic: "0.916", p-value: "1" #> Verdict: cannot reject IV validity at 0.05

title: Getting started with ivcheck
text: iv_test object nine slots: test: human-readable name statistic: observed test statistic (Kolmogorov-Smirnov positive part, scaled sqrt(n)) p_value: bootstrap p-value alpha: level supplied call n_boot: number bootstrap replications used boot_stats: vector bootstrap statistics (useful diagnostics) binding: list identifying (z, z', d, y) configuration observed statistic n: sample size call: matched call full bootstrap distribution retained can draw conclusions different significance level without rerunning simulation:
code: str(result, max.level = 1) #> List of 14 #>  $ test              : chr "Kitagawa (2015)" #>  $ statistic         : num 0.916 #>  $ p_value           : num 1 #>  $ alpha             : num 0.05 #>  $ n_boot            : num 500 #>  $ boot_stats        : num [1:500] 2.47 2.22 3.51 3.02 2.47 ... #>  $ binding           :List of 5 #>  $ weighting         : chr "variance" #>  $ multiplier        : chr "rademacher" #>  $ treatment_order   : chr "ordered" #>  $ n_treatment_levels: int 2 #>  $ multivalued       : logi FALSE #>  $ n                 : int 500 #>  $ call              : language iv_kitagawa.default(y, d, z, n_boot = 500, parallel = FALSE) #>  - attr(*, "class")= chr "iv_test" quantile(result$boot_stats, c(0.9, 0.95, 0.99)) #>      90%      95%      99%  #> 3.176349 3.414021 3.821969

title: Getting started with ivcheck
text: direct effect instrument outcome, conditional treatment, violates exclusion restriction. moderately strong violation Kitagawa statistic starts grow p-value drops. binding configuration tells violation largest: z_low z_high label instrument levels ordered first-stage E[D | Z]. d = 1 says inequality involving treated units violated one. y point outcome grid empirical difference largest.
code: set.seed(2) n <- 1500 z <- sample(0:1, n, replace = TRUE) d <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.3 + 0.4 * z) # Direct Z effect on Y for treated units only: clean exclusion violation y <- rnorm(n, mean = d + 2 * d * z)  result_v <- iv_kitagawa(y, d, z, n_boot = 500, parallel = FALSE) print(result_v) #>  #> ── Kitagawa (2015) ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Sample size: 1500 #> Statistic: "10.7", p-value: "0" #> Verdict: reject IV validity at 0.05 result_v$binding #> $z_low #> [1] 0 #>  #> $z_high #> [1] 1 #>  #> $direction #> [1] "d = 1" #>  #> $y_lower #> [1] -Inf #>  #> $y_upper #> [1] 1.378483

title: Getting started with ivcheck
text: exclusion restriction plausible conditional set covariates, iv_kitagawa misspecified. Use iv_mw() x argument: n_bins controls stratification: iv_mw partitions sample many quantile bins x, runs Kitagawa statistic inside bin, takes maximum across bins joint bootstrap. See vignette("limitations") (?iv_mw) relationship simplified form full Chernozhukov-Lee-Rosen (2013) inference.
code: set.seed(3) n <- 800 x <- rnorm(n) z <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(x)) d <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-0.5 + 0.8 * z + 0.5 * x)) y <- rnorm(n, mean = d + 0.3 * x)  result_mw <- iv_mw(y, d, z, x = x, n_bins = 4, n_boot = 300, parallel = FALSE) print(result_mw) #>  #> ── Mourifie-Wan (2017) ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Sample size: 800 #> Statistic: "29.3", p-value: "0.883" #> Verdict: cannot reject IV validity at 0.05

title: Judge-fixed-effects designs
text: widely-used research design law, labour, public economics instruments binary treatment randomly-assigned identity judge, caseworker, examiner, police officer. Examples include effect pretrial detention defendant outcomes (Dobbie-Goldin-Yang 2018, Leslie-Pope 2017), effect foster care adult outcomes (Doyle 2007), effect disability insurance receipt labour supply (Maestas-Mullen-Strand 2013). identifying assumption judges differ leniency assignment -good--random, Pr(D = 1 | judge) varies across judges E[Y(0), Y(1), D(0), D(1) | judge] . monotonicity, judge’s leniency identifies LATE compliers judge. Frandsen, Lefgren, Leslie (2023, American Economic Review) derive testable implication joint null: E[Y | judge] must linear function E[D | judge]. iv_testjfe() runs test.

title: Judge-fixed-effects designs
code: library(ivcheck)

title: Judge-fixed-effects designs
text: Simulate 20 judges heterogeneous leniency. valid-IV null, Y depends D ; judge identity affects Y effect D. null rejected. fitted slope intercept mu_j p_j close population values (1 0 DGP).
code: set.seed(1) K <- 20 n <- 3000 judge <- sample.int(K, n, replace = TRUE) p_by_j <- seq(0.2, 0.7, length.out = K) d <- rbinom(n, 1, p_by_j[judge]) y <- rnorm(n, mean = d)  r_valid <- iv_testjfe(y, d, judge, n_boot = 100, parallel = FALSE) print(r_valid) #>  #> ── Frandsen-Lefgren-Leslie (2023) ────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Sample size: 3000 #> Statistic: "29.1", p-value: "0.047" #> Verdict: reject IV validity at 0.05

title: Judge-fixed-effects designs
text: Now suppose judge identity affects Y directly, beyond effect D. exclusion violation. test rejects emphatically. direct judge effect Y breaks linearity implication chi-squared--K-2-df asymptotic distribution says observed statistic extremely unlikely null.
code: set.seed(2) # Same first stage, but Y now depends on judge identity non-linearly y_viol <- rnorm(n, mean = d + 1.5 * sin(judge * 0.5))  r_viol <- iv_testjfe(y_viol, d, judge, n_boot = 100, parallel = FALSE) print(r_viol) #>  #> ── Frandsen-Lefgren-Leslie (2023) ────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Sample size: 3000 #> Statistic: "2165", p-value: "0" #> Verdict: reject IV validity at 0.05

title: Judge-fixed-effects designs
text: FLL null, structural model Y = alpha + beta * D + epsilon E[epsilon | judge] = 0. Averaging sides judge: mu_j = alpha + beta * p_j must hold every judge j. iv_testjfe(): Computes per-judge means mu_j propensities p_j. Fits weighted-LS regression mu_j p_j weights equal judge’s sample size. Estimates pooled within-judge variance structural residuals u_i = y_i - alpha_hat - beta_hat * d_i. Computes T_n = sum(n_j * residual_j^2) / sigma^2_hat compares chi^2_{K - 2}. Monte Carlo simulations test suite confirm empirical null distribution matches chi^2_{K - 2} closely (mean, variance, 95th percentile within Monte Carlo error).
code: E[Y | J = j] = alpha + beta * E[D | J = j]

title: Judge-fixed-effects designs
text: binding slot identifies judge largest absolute residual, often useful lead investigating violation originates: fuller picture, plot mu_j p_j fitted line:  valid design, points lie line. violation, wander pattern related direct judge effect.
code: r_viol$binding #> $judge #> [1] 10 #>  #> $mu #> [1] -0.9435573 #>  #> $p #> [1] 0.474359 #>  #> $residual #> [1] -1.656871 judges <- sort(unique(judge)) n_j <- sapply(judges, function(j) sum(judge == j)) p_j <- sapply(judges, function(j) mean(d[judge == j])) mu_j_viol <- sapply(judges, function(j) mean(y_viol[judge == j])) mu_j_valid <- sapply(judges, function(j) mean(y[judge == j]))  oldpar <- par(no.readonly = TRUE) par(mfrow = c(1, 2)) plot(p_j, mu_j_valid, pch = 19, cex = sqrt(n_j) / 5,      main = "Valid design", xlab = "p_j", ylab = "mu_j") abline(lm(mu_j_valid ~ p_j, weights = n_j), col = "red")  plot(p_j, mu_j_viol, pch = 19, cex = sqrt(n_j) / 5,      main = "Exclusion violation", xlab = "p_j", ylab = "mu_j") abline(lm(mu_j_viol ~ p_j, weights = n_j), col = "red") par(oldpar)

title: Judge-fixed-effects designs
text: design plausible conditional covariates (e.g. case characteristics correlate judge assignment chance), pass via x. function residualises y d x computing per-judge means:
code: set.seed(1) K <- 15 n <- 2000 judge <- sample.int(K, n, replace = TRUE) x_case <- rnorm(n) p_by_j <- seq(0.2, 0.7, length.out = K) d <- rbinom(n, 1, p_by_j[judge]) y <- rnorm(n, mean = d + 0.5 * x_case)  r_x <- iv_testjfe(y, d, judge, x = x_case,                   n_boot = 100, parallel = FALSE) print(r_x) #>  #> ── Frandsen-Lefgren-Leslie (2023) ────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Sample size: 2000 #> Statistic: "19.5", p-value: "0.108" #> Verdict: cannot reject IV validity at 0.05

title: Tests for Instrumental Variable Validity
text: ivcheck R package tests identifying assumptions behind instrumental variable (IV) estimation. provides three published falsification tests named R functions, S3 methods fitted fixest ivreg models plus one-shot wrapper runs every applicable test single call. Every applied IV paper rests two assumptions instrument Z: exclusion restriction (Z affects outcome Y endogenous treatment D) monotonicity (defiers). assumptions plus independence, IV estimand identifies local average treatment effect (LATE) compliers (Imbens Angrist 1994). assumptions untestable-looking principle, methodological literature derived testable implications joint distribution (Y, D, Z): Kitagawa (2015), Mourifie-Wan (2017), Frandsen-Lefgren-Leslie (2023). Rejection tests evidence least one exclusion monotonicity failed. Non-rejection evidence detectable violation chosen level. Applied IV research adopted tests widely. empirical IV papers still argue identification narrative (“instrument random-looking X”), referees increasingly frustrated . limiting factor tooling rather conviction: Kitagawa’s test ships supplementary Matlab code, Mourifie-Wan relies Stata clrtest module, Frandsen-Lefgren-Leslie ships Stata SSC module called testjfe. None R. ivcheck closes gap: two added lines fixest::feols call published falsification test ready paper’s appendix.

title: Tests for Instrumental Variable Validity
text: R ecosystem IV estimation mature. fixest dominant package fast fixed-effects IV estimation via feols(y ~ x | d ~ z). ivreg provides classical 2SLS Wu-Hausman, Sargan, weak-IV F tests. ivmodel covers k-class estimators weak-IV-robust confidence intervals. ivDiag (Lal, Lockhart, Xu, Zu 2024, Political Analysis) implements effective-F Anderson-Rubin diagnostics, valid-t local--zero tests, plus sensitivity analysis. None packages implements LATE-validity family falsification tests. Applied researchers want IV design formally tested choose writing one-replication script original paper’s methodology section, switching Stata test back R rest analysis, running test . third option dominated. ivcheck first R-native implementation LATE-validity family. implementations faithful published statistics: Kitagawa’s variance-weighted interval-sup Kolmogorov-Smirnov form (equation 2.1 paper), full Chernozhukov-Lee-Rosen intersection-bounds inference Andrews-Soares adaptive moment selection Mourifie-Wan covariates, asymptotic chi-squared form Frandsen-Lefgren-Leslie multivalued-treatment support via section 4 paper. designed slot existing fixest ivreg workflows without friction.

title: Tests for Instrumental Variable Validity
text: iv_mw() covariates estimates F(y, d | X = x, Z = z) cubic-polynomial series regression, computes heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors, takes sup studentised positive-part violation grid (y, x) points. title: Tests for Instrumental Variable Validity
text: Continuous instruments. three tests require discrete Z. continuous instruments, discretise quantile bins (quartiles quintiles) passing iv_kitagawa iv_mw. formal nonparametric continuous-Z extension v0.2.0 roadmap. Fuzzy regression discontinuity. FRD testable implications cutoff (Arai, Hsu, Kitagawa, Mourifie, Wan 2022). Handling requires different infrastructure (running variable, bandwidth selection, bias correction) fit current fitted-IV-model spine; dedicated iv_frd() function planned v0.2.0. iv_mw covariates weights. weights argument fully implemented iv_kitagawa, iv_testjfe, -covariate path iv_mw. CLR series-regression path iv_mw covariates yet propagate weights; planned v0.1.1. Fixed-effects IV models. iv_kitagawa, iv_mw, iv_testjfe dispatched fixest model | FE | aborts clear error. discrete-Z tests operate raw (Y, D, Z) joint distribution; within-FE residualisation destroys discrete structure Z. Workaround: pre-demean Y D inside FE cell pass raw vectors default method. proper stratified--FE variant v0.2.0 roadmap. Multivariate conditioning iv_mw. conditional path supports single covariate. tensor-product basis multivariate x planned v0.2.0. Multivariate x aborts rather silently dropping additional columns. Sun (2023) unordered multivalued D. Supported via treatment_order = "unordered" plus user-supplied monotonicity_set (data frame columns d, z_from, z_to encoding direction monotonicity restriction per Sun’s Assumption 2.4(iii)). See ?iv_kitagawa example.

what: Notes on fidelity to the published tests
title: Tests for Instrumental Variable Validity
text: Ordered multivalued D tests richer family implications Sun (2023) equation 10. Sun’s Lemma 2.1 derives testable implications using d_min d_max across adjacent Z pairs; ivcheck tests cumulative-tail inequalities P(Y <= y, D <= ell | Z) P(Y <= y, D >= ell | Z) every intermediate level every Z pair. inequalities tested hold Sun’s Assumption 2.2, test valid; stronger (-exhaustive) form Sun’s test rather exact port. se_floor = 0.15 default. Kitagawa (2015) informally recommends xi [0.05, 0.10]. targeted 500-replication Monte Carlo worst design 24-cell grid (n = 300, balanced first stage P(D = 1 | Z) = 0.5, skewed Z 65/35) gives empirical size 11.0% xi = 0.07, 11.0% xi = 0.10, 6.4% xi = 0.15. Kitagawa’s recommended range anti-conservative factor 2 regime; raise default 0.15 evidence. Users wanting reproduce Kitagawa’s published examples pass se_floor = 0.1 expect -rejection small n skewed Z. iv_testjfe implements FLL (2023) “fit” component optional flexible basis. Pass basis_order = 1 (default) Sargan-Hansen overidentification form (constant treatment effects); pass basis_order > 1 polynomial phi(p) = delta_0 + delta_1 p + ... + delta_m p^m matching FLL’s richer specification. binary D supported basis_order > 1. slope-bounded moment-inequality component FLL test (Andrews-Soares 2010 inference LATE-support inequality) deferred v0.2.0; users needing full published test run Frandsen’s Stata testjfe module interim. iv_kitagawa ordered-multivalued path now includes marginal P(D <= c | Z) stochastic-dominance contribution Sun (2023) equation 10 (second inequality). test statistic maximum joint (Y, D | Z) sup marginal D | Z cumulative-distribution shift across adjacent Z pairs. iv_mw CLR path independent construction, port Mourifie-Wan’s Matlab code. Series-regression basis, sandwich variance, multiplier bootstrap Andrews-Soares moment selection follow CLR framework. Cross-validation authors’ code v0.2.0 wishlist. Multiplier bootstrap defaults Rademacher weights. multiplier = "gaussian" multiplier = "mammen" also available. Kitagawa’s Sun’s papers use multiplier-type bootstraps; Sun explicitly uses Rademacher.

title: Tests for Instrumental Variable Validity
text: Non-rejection proof validity. tests power violations observable conditional distributions silent violations cancel across subgroups. Kitagawa vs Mourifie-Wan covariates. exclusion restriction plausible conditional X, run iv_mw x. Running iv_kitagawa unconditionally X-dependent design can give spurious non-rejection. Many-instrument / judge regimes. 20+ judge levels, prefer iv_testjfe iv_kitagawa; KS test loses power rapidly |Z| grows. Bootstrap size. n_boot = 1000 (default) fine publication-grade p-values. Cut 200 exploration; raise 5000 reporting p-values three decimal places. se_floor trimming constant (Kitagawa’s \xi) material impact finite-sample size. default 0.15, raised paper’s informally-recommended 0.05-0.1 range Monte Carlo showed smaller floors produce anti-conservative size skewed Z-cell distributions weak first stages. 0.15 empirical size nominal 5% 24 Monte Carlo configurations tested. Users reproducing Kitagawa’s published examples can set se_floor = 0.1.

title: Tests for Instrumental Variable Validity
text: Kitagawa statistic matches equation 2.1 paper. sup taken full class intervals [y, y'] y <= y', normalised binomial-mixture plug-standard error. variance-weighted form default; unweighted form equation 2.2 available via weighting = "unweighted". iv_mw covariates implements full Chernozhukov-Lee-Rosen (2013) intersection-bounds framework: series-regression conditional CDF estimation, heteroscedasticity-robust plug-standard errors, multiplier bootstrap adaptive moment selection. Without covariates, iv_mw reduces exactly variance-weighted Kitagawa test (unit-tested). iv_testjfe null distribution approximately chi^2_{K-2} finite-sample conservatism. K=20, N=3000 200 replications: empirical mean 17.7 vs target 18.0, variance 28.8 vs target 36.0, 95th percentile 27.5 vs target 28.9. Empirical size nominal 5%: 1.5% (asymptotic method), 2.5% (method = "bootstrap"). conservatism arises estimated judge propensities hat p_j entering test regressors: finite-sample binomial variance hat p_j n_j = 150 per judge compresses reference distribution asymptotic chi-squared. approximation sharpens n_j grows. method = "bootstrap" recommended publication-grade p-values modest n_j. DOIs CrossRef-verified. pre-release audit caught silent bug: Mourifie-Wan’s DOI cited 10.1162/REST_a_00628 (resolves different paper entirely). correct DOI _00622. Fixed first submission. R CMD check ---cran: 0 errors, 0 warnings, 0 notes. 97 unit tests covering structure, invariants, known-value cases, edge cases, end--end S3 dispatch fixest ivreg fitted models.

code: # \donttest{ if (requireNamespace("fixest", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(1)   n <- 500   df <- data.frame(     z = sample(0:1, n, replace = TRUE),     x = rnorm(n)   )   df$d <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.3 + 0.4 * df$z)   df$y <- rnorm(n, mean = df$d + 0.5 * df$x)   m <- fixest::feols(y ~ x | d ~ z, data = df)   iv_check(m, n_boot = 200) } #>  #> ── IV validity diagnostic ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Kitagawa (2015): stat = "0.845", p = "1", pass #> Mourifie-Wan (2017): stat = "42.4", p = "0.45", pass #> Overall: cannot reject IV validity at 0.05. # }

what: Kitagawa (2015) / Sun (2023) test for instrument validity — iv_kitagawa
title: Kitagawa (2015) / Sun (2023) test for instrument validity — iv_kitagawa
text: Tests joint implication local exclusion restriction local monotonicity condition discrete-instrument setting. Supports binary treatment (Kitagawa 2015), ordered multivalued treatment (Sun 2023 section 3), unordered multivalued treatment (Sun 2023 section 3.3) user-supplied monotonicity set. null instrument valid. null, conditional joint distribution (Y, D | Z) must satisfy stochastic dominance inequalities cumulative-tail events. Rejection evidence least one exclusion monotonicity fails.

title: Kitagawa (2015) / Sun (2023) test for instrument validity — iv_kitagawa
text: object default method: numeric outcome vector. fixest ivreg methods: fitted instrumental variable model fixest::feols ivreg::ivreg(). ... arguments passed methods. d Binary 0/1 treatment vector (default method ). z Discrete instrument (numeric factor, default method ). n_boot Number multiplier-bootstrap replications. Default 1000. alpha Significance level returned verdict. Default 0.05. weighting Test-statistic weighting. "variance" (default) divides pointwise difference plug-standard error estimator taking sup, Kitagawa (2015) section 4. "unweighted" uses raw positive-part KS section 3. two asymptotically equivalent boundary null; "variance" better finite-sample power instrument cells unequal sizes. weights Optional survey weights. non-negative numeric vector length equal sample size. Scaled internally mean weight 1.0 (preserving effective sample-size interpretation). Applied empirical CDFs, bootstrap multiplier process, variance-weighted standard errors. parallel Logical. Run bootstrap replications parallel POSIX systems via parallel::mclapply. Default TRUE. se_floor Trimming constant \xi plug-standard- error denominator variance-weighted form. Default 0.15. Kitagawa (2015) section 4 informally recommends \xi \[0.05, 0.1] balanced-Z designs. Monte Carlo skewed Z-cell distributions weak first stages suggests slightly larger floor (0.15) keeps empirical size near nominal 5% without measurable power loss designs tested. Users reproducing Kitagawa's published examples may set se_floor = 0.1 match. treatment_order Either "ordered" (default) "unordered". Binary D handled identically . multivalued D, "ordered" uses cumulative-tail inequalities P(Y <= y, D <= ell | Z) P(Y <= y, D >= ell | Z) across pairs instrument values, stronger family implications Sun (2023) equation 10's d_min--d_max subset (still valid Sun's Assumption 2.2). "unordered" requires user-specified monotonicity_set naming (level, z_from, z_to) triples 1{D_{z_to} = d} <= 1{D_{z_from} = d} assumed almost surely (Sun 2023 Assumption 2.4(iii)). monotonicity_set data.frame columns d, z_from, z_to listing triples pin direction monotonicity restriction treatment_order = "unordered". Ignored treatment_order = "ordered". multiplier Choice bootstrap multiplier: "rademacher" (default; +/-1 two-point), "gaussian" (standard normal), "mammen" (Mammen 1993 asymmetric two-point).

title: Kitagawa (2015) / Sun (2023) test for instrument validity — iv_kitagawa

title: Kitagawa (2015) / Sun (2023) test for instrument validity — iv_kitagawa
text: Kitagawa (2015) equation 2.1 defines statistic max instrument-level pairs (z_low, z_high), treatment status d {0, 1}, intervals [y, y'] y <= y', positive-part interval-probability difference normalised binomial-mixture plug-standard error: T_n = sqrt(n_low * n_high / (n_low + n_high)) * max [P([y, y'], d | z_low) - P([y, y'], d | z_high)]^+ / sigma_hat. (denominator pair total, full sample size.) sign flips d = 0. Instrument levels pre-ordered first-stage E_hat[D | Z] inequalities one-sided T_n >= 0. implementation evaluates sup quantile grid observed outcomes (default 50 points); equivalent evaluation every sample-point pair Kitagawa's Theorem 2.1. Critical values come multiplier bootstrap (section 3.2) pooled empirical distribution; bootstrap statistics reuse data-derived standard-error denominator.

what: Mourifie-Wan (2017) test for instrument validity — iv_mw
title: Mourifie-Wan (2017) test for instrument validity — iv_mw
text: Reformulates testable implications Kitagawa (2015) set conditional moment inequalities tests intersection- bounds framework Chernozhukov, Lee, Rosen (2013). Without covariates x, iv_mw tests inequalities iv_kitagawa reduces exactly variance-weighted Kitagawa test. covariates, iv_mw estimates conditional CDFs F(y, d | X = x, Z = z) nonparametrically via series regression, computes plug-heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors, takes sup (y, x) variance-weighted positive-part violation. Critical values come multiplier bootstrap adaptive moment selection style Andrews Soares (2010).

NULL, test reduces unconditional Mourifie-Wan test. basis_order Polynomial order series-regression basis used estimate F(y, d | X, Z). Default 3L (cubic). Set "auto" select basis order 5-fold cross-validation candidates 2, 3, 4, 5 squared-error loss indicator regression. title: Mourifie-Wan (2017) test for instrument validity — iv_mw
text: CLR framework targets conditional moment inequalities form E[m(W; theta) | X] <= 0 X. Applied Kitagawa's (2015) inequalities, relevant moments positive-part differences conditional joint CDFs F(y, d | X, Z) (d, z_low, z_high, y, x) index. iv_mw estimates F(y, d | X, Z) series regression indicator 1{Y <= y, D = d} polynomial basis X within Z cell. Robust standard errors come heteroscedasticity-consistent sandwich series regression. Critical values drawn multiplier bootstrap: bootstrap process reuses plug-SE denominator perturbs residuals Rademacher weights, projected back basis. Adaptive moment selection includes moments whose observed studentised statistic within kappa_n inequality boundary, giving tighter critical values inequalities strictly slack.

text: object default method: numeric outcome vector. fixest ivreg methods: fitted instrumental variable model fixest::feols ivreg::ivreg(). ... arguments passed methods. d Binary 0/1 treatment vector (default method ). z Factor, integer, matrix mutually exclusive dummy variables identifying judge (random-assignment unit). x Optional numeric vector, matrix, data frame covariates. supplied, y d residualised x per- judge means computed. n_boot Number multiplier-bootstrap replications. Default 1000. alpha Significance level returned verdict. Default 0.05. method Reference distribution p-value. "asymptotic" (default) uses chi-squared K - (basis_order + 1) degrees freedom. "bootstrap" uses multiplier bootstrap restricted-model residual process. Asymptotic fast accurate moderate K; bootstrap preferred small K errors far normal. weights Optional survey weights. non-negative numeric vector length equal sample size. Scaled internally mean weight 1.0 (preserving effective sample-size interpretation). Applied empirical CDFs, bootstrap multiplier process, variance-weighted standard errors. basis_order Order polynomial basis used approximate outcome / propensity function phi(p) Frandsen-Lefgren-Leslie (2023) step 1. Default 1L reduces Sargan-Hansen overidentification form, imposes constant treatment effects. Values 1 relax phi(p) = delta_0 + delta_1 p + delta_2 p^2 + ... + delta_m p^m test joint-zero restriction judge residuals richer fit. binary treatment supported basis_order > 1. slope-bounded moment-inequality component FLL test implemented v0.1.0 (deferred v0.2.0). parallel Logical. Run bootstrap replications parallel POSIX systems via parallel::mclapply. Default TRUE.

Additional elements: n_judges Number distinct judges / assignment groups. coef Fitted weighted-LS slope intercept mu_j p_j. pairwise_late K x K matrix pairwise Wald LATE estimates (mu_j - mu_k) / (p_j - p_k). null every entry estimates common complier LATE. worst_pair List identifying judge pair largest deviation Wald LATE fitted slope; useful diagnosing source rejection.

title: Frandsen-Lefgren-Leslie (2023) test for instrument validity in judge-fixed-effects designs — iv_testjfe
text: joint null, pair judges (j, k) identifies complier LATE via Wald estimator (mu_j - mu_k) / (p_j - p_k). Frandsen-Lefgren-Leslie (2023) test overidentification test "pairwise LATEs equal". binary treatment WLS weighting, overidentification test algebraically weighted sum squared residuals linear fit mu_j = alpha + beta * p_j, divided pooled variance estimator. iv_testjfe computes quadratic form , default, compares chi-squared distribution K - 2 degrees freedom (FLL asymptotic form). multiplier bootstrap restricted residual process available via method = "bootstrap" small-K robustness. Note finite-sample size. Per-judge propensities p_j enter test estimated regressors. modest per-judge sample sizes (n_j hundred), finite-sample binomial noise hat p_j compresses distribution test statistic asymptotic chi-squared reference, producing test mildly conservative nominal 5 percent. Empirical size K = 20, N = 3000 1.5 percent asymptotic method 2.5 percent bootstrap. methods sharpen toward nominal n_j grows. bootstrap recommended publication-grade p-values modest n_j. returned object includes pairwise_late, K x K matrix pairwise Wald LATE estimates, worst_pair, judge pair largest absolute deviation fitted slope. diagnostic outputs sense paper's Figure 2: pair whose Wald LATE deviates far common slope first place look investigating rejection. Multivalued treatment supported: D M + 1 distinct values (0, 1, ..., M), fit becomes multiple WLS regression mu_j M-vector (P(D = 1 | J), ..., P(D = M | J)) test statistic compared chi^2_{K - M - 1} (FLL 2023 section 4). pairwise_late worst_pair defined binary D return NULL otherwise.

title: ivcheck 0.1.0
text: First CRAN submission. Three falsification tests identifying assumptions instrumental variable estimation local average treatment effect, plus one-shot wrapper. known deviations published tests (richer family ordered-multivalued inequalities Sun 2023 Lemma 2.1; linearity-test form Frandsen, Lefgren, Leslie 2023; empirical se_floor = 0.15 versus Kitagawa 2015’s 0.05-0.10 recommendation), see “Notes fidelity” sub-section README companion R Journal paper paper/rj/paper.Rmd.

title: ivcheck 0.1.0
text: iv_kitagawa(): Kitagawa (2015) variance-weighted Kolmogorov-Smirnov test instrument validity binary (ordered multivalued, per Sun 2023) treatment discrete instrument. Supports survey weights, user-tunable se_floor trimming, unweighted variance-weighted forms. Returns binding (z, z', d, y) interval users can see outcome region carries violation. iv_mw(): Mourifie Wan (2017) conditional moment-inequality reformulation. Without covariates, reduces exactly variance- weighted Kitagawa test (unit-tested). covariate, uses full Chernozhukov-Lee-Rosen (2013) intersection-bounds inference series-regression conditional CDF estimation, heteroscedasticity- robust plug-standard errors, Andrews-Soares (2010) adaptive moment selection. iv_testjfe(): Frandsen, Lefgren, Leslie (2023) joint test exclusion monotonicity judge-fixed-effects designs. Asymptotic chi-squared multiplier-bootstrap p-values. Supports binary multivalued ordered treatment, survey weights, covariate residualisation. Returns pairwise-Wald-LATE matrix worst deviating pair diagnostics. iv_check(): one-shot wrapper runs applicable tests fitted IV model returns tidy summary overall verdict. iv_power(): Monte Carlo power simulator anchored observed first-stage propensities, sample-size planning benchmarking.
